evidence_quotes.quote: Animations, suspense, and near-miss effects act as dark patterns.
Impose transparency for influencer/creator promotions of cases/skins (clear paid-relationship disclosure) with platform responsibility to enforce.

paragraphs.text: The gaming sectorespecially Valve Corporations Steam ecosystemreveals gaps in EU rules. Valves monetization systems, including loot boxes, skin gambling mechanics, and the Steam Community Market, combine manipulative design, addictive loops, and opaque trading that expose consumersespecially minorsto financial and psychological harm. Manipulative and addictive design in loot boxes Counter-Strike 2 and other titles use randomized case opening systems that mirror gambling reward schedules. Variable-ratio reinforcement drives repeated purchases to chase rare items. Animations, suspense, and near-miss effects act as dark patterns. Probability information can be hard to find, and users (including minors) lack simple tools to track or limit spending. These practices match the DFAs concerns about manipulative interfaces, addictive design, and protection of vulnerable users. EU rules should require clear probability disclosures, spending dashboards, per-session and monthly limits (stricter defaults for minors), and interfaces that avoid pressure tactics. Market integrity and insider-abuse risks The Steam Community Market enables trading of digital items with real monetary value. Before some updates, trading surges that later align with the most profitable post-update moves. Repeated at scale, this suggests information asymmetries or coordinated prediction beyond what ordinary consumers can match. While not conclusive proof of insider dealing, the outcome is similar: a subset captures gains around market-moving events while typical users bear the risk. There is no public evidence of robust internal controls at Valveconflict-of-interest rules, audited access logs, or routine market-abuse reportingto prevent privileged access from influencing trading or to rule it out. High-value items can trade for thousands of euros without transparency comparable to financial or collectibles markets, leaving consumers exposed to imbalances and speculative losses. Why EU action is necessary These practices are cross-border. National measures alone create fragmentation and uneven protection. The DFA can set harmonized, enforceable standards that apply equally to EU and non-EU platforms serving EU users. Recommended measures Cover loot boxes, random-reward mechanics, and digital item markets under DFA provisions on manipulative/addictive design. Mandate standardized probability disclosures at purchase; per-account spending dashboards; configurable caps; and default limits and cooling-off for minors. Require market-integrity safeguards: insider-abuse controls (access controls, conflict-of-interest rules, anomaly surveillance), tamper-proof audit logs, independent audits, and cooperation with authorities. Ensure age verification, parental consent for under-18s, easy cancellation/refund, and opt-outs from purchase prompts. Impose transparency for influencer/creator promotions of cases/skins (clear paid-relationship disclosure) with platform responsibility to enforce. Add event-risk controls around updates (staff trading embargoes, time-stamped change logs) and continuous surveillance for unusual pre-update trading that profits after releases. Establish cross-border enforcement with penalties that deter violations and consistent consumer redress. Conclusion Valves ecosystem shows how entertainment, speculation, and gambling-like mechanics can converge on a large, lightly regulated platform. Without explicit DFA rules, EU consumersespecially young usersremain exposed to addictive design, market manipulation, and information exploitation. The DFA should recognize loot boxes and digital item markets as high-risk practices, require transparent, non-manipulative interfaces, and impose market-integrity controls that prevent insider abuse. This will protect vulnerable consumers, strengthen trust, and support a fair, competitive, unified digital single market.
